role_summary: First African-American Dean of Students (1997) and first African-American Assistant Vice President for Student Affairs at the University of Southern Mississippi; first elected African-American Hattiesburg City Council member (1985-2000); doctor of educational administration; forty-year USM administrator (1979-2019).
ai_reading: Holloway's top semantic neighbor is Robert L. Carter (entry 108) at a cosine count of 102; the broader cluster includes Harold K. Brown (entry 55), Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew (entry 16), and Clarence Magee (entry 21). The Carter pairing is the catalog's clearest legal-administration-and-college-administration cosine, two senior institutional administrators whose vocabularies the embedding finds rhyming despite the different fields (federal judiciary vs higher-education student affairs). The novel observation his page surfaces is the witnessed-event vs participated-event distinction: Holloway describes himself as a first-hand witness to the 1964 Freedom Summer in Hattiesburg rather than as a movement organizer, and the embedding catches that observational register, clustering him with administrators who frame the civil rights work institutionally rather than with the front-line SNCC organizers his geography would otherwise predict.
biographical_paragraph: While Holloway's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), his external biographical record is well-documented through the University of Southern Mississippi institutional archives and Hattiesburg local press [src: 1]. Holloway grew up in segregated Hattiesburg, Mississippi during the Freedom Summer and Voting Rights era, and entered the University of Southern Mississippi as a freshman in fall 1970 as part of the early post-integration cohort [src: 2]. He earned four degrees from USM: a Bachelor's in sociology and personnel management, a Master's in community counseling, a second Master's in public health education, and a doctorate in educational administration [src: 2]. After early work at William Carey University and in alcohol and drug treatment facilities he returned to USM in 1979 and remained for forty years, becoming in 1997 the first African American Dean of Students and the first African American Assistant Vice President for Student Affairs at the university, before retiring in 2019 [src: 2]. He also served as the first elected African American member of the Hattiesburg City Council (1985-2000), holding terms as council president and vice president across that fifteen-year tenure [src: 2]. He has subsequently joined the Hattiesburg Public School District Board of Trustees.

interview_snippets.speaker: Eddie Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: After his mother died when he was eleven, Holloway spent summers with an aunt and uncle in Bessemer, Alabama, twelve miles from Birmingham, in the years following the 1963 demonstrations there. He describes what they taught him:
interview_snippets.quote: They instilled in me a heaping dose of pride, pride in person, pride in appearance, pride in the speech decor, never borrow, never request, never be, put your best foot forward every day.

interview_snippets.speaker: Eddie Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked what he thought about the segregation and voting restrictions he grew up under in Hattiesburg, Holloway separates accepting a present condition from accepting it as permanent:
interview_snippets.quote: I accepted that Black folk couldn't vote. I accepted that there were separate facilities. I didn't accept that that would be permanent, but I accepted that that was the status quo. I accepted that that would be those that would agree to that. But I knew that that wouldn't be permanent. That in time, things would change.

interview_snippets.speaker: Eddie Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Holloway describes his own participation in the Hattiesburg marches and his decision to withdraw when he judged a situation dangerous:
interview_snippets.quote: I marched. When I was since danger, I would leave. If we were marching one night up town and we get there, and I see people that represented the clans with guns. And I knew all the other guys with me on there, a couple of bottles, broken a beer bottle. I'd go home.

interview_snippets.speaker: Eddie Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked what he wanted people to understand about his role, Holloway frames his work as inside negotiation rather than public demonstration:
interview_snippets.quote: I think we, in my mind, people that were on the inside of the civil rights movement, maybe were less heralded, but were able to negotiate and accomplish far more than maybe many that had great posture and absorbed the television screens.

interview_snippets.speaker: Eddie Holloway
interview_snippets.lead_in: Holloway describes the approach to cross-racial relationships he carried into his work at USM and on the Hattiesburg City Council:
interview_snippets.quote: I say you build a bridge. And it may not be strong enough for me to go across it, but maybe tomorrow someone else can.

interview_snippets.speaker: Clarence Magee
interview_snippets.lead_in: Clarence Magee, longtime Forrest County NAACP president and Holloway's tenth-grade science teacher, recounts his continuing connection to Holloway as a USM administrator:
interview_snippets.quote: And of course, since that time, now I still have a good relationship with Dr. Eddie Holloway and other persons that I hear.

movement_context: The Hattiesburg Holloway grew up in was the operational center of Mississippi voter-registration organizing across the mid-1960s. Hattiesburg sat in Forrest County, which had become a national focal point of the franchise fight after the long-running federal voting-rights litigation against Forrest County registrar Theron Lynd produced a March 1962 contempt finding, the first such judgment against a Mississippi registrar [src: 3, 4]. The Council of Federated Organizations (COFO), the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), and the NAACP-linked Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party concentrated Freedom Summer 1964 volunteers in Hattiesburg in larger numbers than any other Mississippi city, producing the January 22, 1964 Freedom Day mass mobilization at the Forrest County Courthouse and the summer's Freedom Schools network operating out of local Black churches and the Mt. Zion Baptist hall [src: 3, 4]. The Forrest County NAACP chapter that anchored this work passed from Vernon Dahmer Sr.'s leadership through the trauma of his January 10, 1966 murder by White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan, an attack that followed Dahmer Sr.'s January 9, 1966 WDAM-TV announcement that his Kelly Settlement general store would accept poll-tax payments on behalf of Black registrants too fearful to enter the Forrest County Courthouse [src: 5]. The chapter then continued under his widow Ellie Dahmer and, beginning in 1970, under Clarence Magee (catalog entry 21), whose first 1970-1974 presidency overlapped with Holloway's USM freshman cohort and whose 2001-present second tenure has covered Holloway's post-USM-administrator decades [src: 5]. Holloway entered USM in fall 1970, six years after the assassination of state NAACP field secretary Medgar Evers, six years after Freedom Summer, four-and-a-half years after the Dahmer murder, and five years after the Voting Rights Act of 1965 federal-registrar provision had finally compelled Forrest County to register Black voters at the courthouse [src: 4, 5]. The post-1965 youth-chapter substrate Holloway came of age inside was not the front-line SNCC organizing of the early-1960s mobilization phase but the institutional-consolidation phase that followed: voter education, school-desegregation negotiation, and the slow integration of previously all-white state institutions like USM [src: 3, 6].
legacy_and_reception: Holloway's institutional record at USM is documented through the university's archival press releases and student-publication retrospectives. His 1997 elevation to Dean of Students made him the first African American to hold that role at USM, thirty-two years after the September 1965 court-ordered desegregation of the university under the federal injunction that admitted Raylawni Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong as USM's first Black undergraduate students [src: 2, 7]. His subsequent appointment as the first African American Assistant Vice President for Student Affairs cemented the role of African American administrators in USM's central student-services leadership through the late 1990s and into the 2010s [src: 2]. His 1985-2000 fifteen-year tenure on the Hattiesburg City Council, as the first elected African American member of that body, ran concurrently with the city's broader transition from the legally-segregated municipal governance of the early 1960s to the present majority-Black-population Hattiesburg of the 2020 federal census, the institutional arc the council seat tracked across that period [src: 2, 8]. His subsequent service on the Hattiesburg Public School District Board of Trustees extends the school-desegregation-and-integration work that Clarence Magee and other Forrest County NAACP leaders had begun in the 1960s and 1970s into the present [src: 2].

gallery.alt: College Hall, University of Southern Mississippi, Hattiesburg, November 2016
gallery.caption: College Hall at the University of Southern Mississippi in Hattiesburg, the campus where Holloway earned four degrees (Bachelor's in 1974, two Master's, and a doctorate in educational administration) and served as a senior student-affairs administrator from 1979 through his 2019 retirement. The 1965 federal-court-ordered desegregation of USM admitted Raylawni Branch and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong as the university's first Black undergraduate students; Holloway entered with the fall 1970 cohort, five years into the university's post-desegregation period.

gallery.alt: Ellie Dahmer, widow of Vernon Dahmer Sr., assisted to a car by family members at his funeral, Shady Grove Baptist Church, Kelly Settlement, Hattiesburg, Mississippi, January 15, 1966
gallery.caption: Ellie Dahmer, widow of Forrest County NAACP president Vernon Dahmer Sr., assisted to a car by family members at his funeral service held at Shady Grove Baptist Church in Kelly Settlement near Hattiesburg on January 15, 1966, five days after his death from smoke-inhalation injuries sustained in the January 10 White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan firebombing of his home and general store. Ellie Dahmer carried the Forrest County NAACP chapter through the post-1966 transition; Clarence Magee (catalog entry 21) succeeded to the presidency in 1970, the same year Holloway entered USM as a freshman. Photograph from the Mississippi Department of Archives and History Moncrief Photograph Collection.

sources.title: Patricia Sullivan, Lift Every Voice: The NAACP and the Making of the Civil Rights Movement
sources.publisher: The New Press, 2009; WorldCat OCLC 41368421 (Sullivan's institutional history of the NAACP including the Mississippi state-conference work of the 1950s and 1960s and the federal litigation that produced the 1962 Theron Lynd contempt finding)

sources.title: Curtis Wilkie, Dixie: A Personal Odyssey Through Events That Shaped the Modern South
sources.publisher: Scribner, 2001; WorldCat OCLC 61247268 (the Wilkie chapters on the January 1966 Vernon Dahmer Sr. murder and the long-arc Bowers prosecutions through the 1990s)

sources.title: Justice Delivered: The Vernon Dahmer Case
sources.publisher: Federal Bureau of Investigation, May 12, 2008 (FBI institutional retrospective on the 1998 Samuel Bowers federal conviction for the January 1966 Dahmer murder, with chronology of the Forrest County NAACP-led poll-tax payment program that preceded the Klan attack)

sources.title: John Dittmer, Local People: The Struggle for Civil Rights in Mississippi
sources.publisher: University of Illinois Press, 1994; WorldCat OCLC 37464286 (Dittmer's state-level history of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, the COFO Hattiesburg project, and the Forrest County NAACP youth-chapter substrate)
